lesson:
Japanese has three words for "why" and they sit on a politeness ladder. なぜ is at the top — formal, composed, the kind of "why" you'd use in writing or when speaking carefully. どうして is the middle — everyday, neutral, the most common in conversation. なんで is the bottom — casual, sometimes plaintive or frustrated.

なぜですか (why is that?) sounds measured and intellectual. どうして？ sounds like a genuine question. なんで！？ sounds like you're upset. Choose wrong and the emotional temperature of your sentence shifts dramatically.

なぜ comes from 何ぜ — 何 (what) + ぜ (an archaic particle expressing reason). "What reason?" compressed into two syllables. You'll see it written in kanji occasionally in formal texts, but hiragana is standard.

The kanji 並 shows two people standing side by side — two identical figures, shoulder to shoulder. That visual is the word: things arranged in a line, in a row, in parallel. 人が並んでいる (people are lined up). 本が並んでいる (books are lined up on a shelf).

並ぶ is intransitive — things line themselves up. Its transitive partner 並べる (ならべる) means you arrange things in a row. 子供が並んだ (the kids lined up) vs 子供を並べた (I lined the kids up).

In Japan, queuing is an art form. 並ぶ is what you do at ramen shops, theme parks, train platforms. 並んでください (please line up) is a phrase you'll hear from staff everywhere. The Japanese queue is orderly by default — the word 並ぶ carries that expectation of neatness built in.

Particles: 並ぶ takes に for where you line up: 列に並ぶ (line up in a row). Also が for what's lined up: 店が並んでいる (shops are lined up along the street).

Passive に並ばれる: rare for this intransitive verb. In figurative use: 彼に並ばれた (he drew level with me — matched my level, which I didn't want).

Causative を／に並ばせる: making someone line up. を for coercive: 生徒を並ばせる (make the students line up).

五段 Godan自動詞 — pair: 並べる (ならべる)
Dictionaryならぶに
Negativeならばないに
Politeならびますに
Polite negならびませんに
Pastならんだに
Polite pastならびましたに
て-formならんでに
Volitionalならぼうに
Polite volならびましょうに
Passive
Causativeならばせるを／に
Caus-passならばせられるに
Potentialならべるに
Imperativeならべに
Imp なさいならびなさいに
Cond ～ばならべばに
Cond ～たらならんだらに

運 has the road radical (辻) with 軍 (army, military) inside. An army on the move, carrying supplies along the road. That's the core image: physically transporting something from one place to another. 荷物を運ぶ (carry luggage). 料理を運ぶ (serve food — carry dishes to the table).

The figurative extension is elegant: 足を運ぶ literally means "carry your feet" — to make the trip, to go somewhere. 何度も足を運んだ (I went there many times — I carried my feet there repeatedly). It implies deliberate effort, not casual dropping by.

The same kanji appears in 運動 (exercise, movement) and 運転 (driving) — both involve moving things along a path. And 運 also means luck/fortune (運がいい = good luck), probably from the idea that your fate is carried along by forces you can't control.

Particles: 運ぶ takes を — the thing you carry is the direct object. 荷物を運ぶ. Use に for the destination: 部屋に運ぶ (carry to the room).

Potential が運べる: が — what can be carried becomes the subject. これが一人で運べる？ (can this be carried by one person?).

Passive に運ばれる: に marks the carrier. 病院に運ばれた (was carried to the hospital).

Causative に…を運ばせる: に marks who you make carry, を marks what. 後輩に荷物を運ばせる (make the junior carry the luggage).

五段 Godan他動詞
Dictionaryはこぶを
Negativeはこばないを
Politeはこびますを
Polite negはこびませんを
Pastはこんだを
Polite pastはこびましたを
て-formはこんでを
Volitionalはこぼうを
Polite volはこびましょうを
Passiveはこばれるに
Causativeはこばせるに／を
Caus-passはこばせられるに
Potentialはこべるが
Imperativeはこべを
Imp なさいはこびなさいを
Cond ～ばはこべばを
Cond ～たらはこんだらを

直 means straight, honest, direct — and also to fix, correct, heal. The core idea: returning something to its proper state. What's broken gets straightened. What's wrong gets righted. 直す is the transitive form: you fix it.

直す vs 治す: Both read なおす, both mean "to fix/cure." 直す is for objects and abstract things — fixing a machine, correcting a mistake, redoing homework. 治す is for illness and injury — curing a disease, healing a wound. Broken bike? 直す. Broken arm? 治す.

Pattern unlock: 〜直す attached to a verb's stem means "redo": 書き直す (rewrite), やり直す (redo), 読み直す (reread). This is enormously useful. Any verb can become a "re-" verb by adding 直す.

Particles: 直す takes を — the broken thing is the direct object. 車を直す (fix the car).

Potential が直せる: が — what can be fixed becomes the subject. これが直せる？ (can this be fixed?).

Passive に直される: に marks who does the fixing. 先生に作文を直された (the teacher corrected my essay).

Causative に…を直させる: に marks who you make fix it, を marks what. 子供に自転車を直させる (make the kid fix the bicycle).

五段 Godan他動詞 — pair: 直る (なおる)
Dictionaryなおすを
Negativeなおさないを
Politeなおしますを
Polite negなおしませんを
Pastなおしたを
Polite pastなおしましたを
て-formなおしてを
Volitionalなおそうを
Polite volなおしましょうを
Passiveなおされるに
Causativeなおさせるに／を
Caus-passなおさせられるに
Potentialなおせるが
Imperativeなおせを
Imp なさいなおしなさいを
Cond ～ばなおせばを
Cond ～たらなおしたらを

反 (reverse, anti-) + 対 (against, facing). Reversed and facing against — opposition in its purest form. The word works as a noun (反対がある, there's opposition), a する-verb (反対する, to oppose), and an adjective (反対の意見, an opposing opinion).

But 反対 also means "opposite" in a spatial or logical sense: 反対側 (はんたいがわ, the opposite side). 反対方向 (the opposite direction). So when someone says 反対, context determines whether they mean "I disagree" or "the other way around."

In democratic discourse, 賃成 (さんせい, in favor) and 反対 are the two sides of every vote. 賃成ですか、反対ですか (are you for or against?) is a question structure worth memorizing whole.

場 (place, scene) + 合 (fit, match, combine). The place where circumstances combine — a situation, a case. It's the Japanese equivalent of "in the case of" or "in the event that."

This word is a conditional workhorse. 雨の場合 (in case of rain). 必要な場合 (if necessary — "in the case that it's needed"). その場合 (in that case). Unlike とき (when, at the time), 場合 emphasizes uncertainty — it's about hypothetical scenarios, things that might or might not happen.

You'll see it everywhere in instructions, rules, and formal communication: 火事の場合はこの出口を使ってください (in case of fire, use this exit). It's the word that turns general statements into conditional ones.

詳しい does double duty. It can describe information (詳しい説明 a detailed explanation) or a person (彼は日本の歴史に詳しい he's knowledgeable about Japanese history). "Detailed" when modifying things, "well-informed" when describing people. Both senses orbit the same idea: thoroughness, depth of knowledge.

The particle matters when describing a person: に marks the topic they know about. コンピューターに詳しい (knowledgeable about computers). Drop the に and it shifts to describing the information itself: 詳しいこと (detailed things / the specifics).

詳しくは… (for details...) is a useful transitional phrase. 詳しくはウェブサイトをご覧ください (for details, please see the website). The adverb form 詳しく smoothly introduces elaboration.

Dictionaryくわしい
Negativeくわしくない
Pastくわしかった
Past negくわしくなかった
Adverbくわしく
て-formくわしくて

No kanji in common use — it's almost always written in hiragana. The word covers a range from harmless childhood pranks to more serious misconduct, depending on context. 子供のいたずら is cute mischief — hiding someone's shoes, drawing on a wall. いたずら電話 (prank call) is less cute.

The word comes from classical Japanese 徒ら (いたづら), which originally meant "pointless, useless" — doing something with no productive purpose. The evolution from "purposeless" to "mischievous" makes sense: a prank is an act with no useful goal except causing a reaction.

いたずらする is the verb form. いたずら好き (someone who loves pranks). In Japanese schools, いたずら ranges from the kind of thing teachers sigh about to something that borders on いじめ (bullying) — the line between the two is a constant cultural negotiation.

The お prefix here isn't optional politeness — it's fused to the word. While 祝い exists on its own (from the verb 祝う, to celebrate), you'll almost always hear the お version. Taking it off sounds curt, like handing someone a birthday card and grunting "congrats" instead of "congratulations."

The kanji 祝 has the altar radical 示 (originally a picture of a stone altar with offerings dripping down it) next to 兄, which here represents a person kneeling with arms raised in prayer. A person at an altar, giving thanks to the gods. That's the oldest layer of 祝い — not a party, but a sacred rite of gratitude. The festive meaning grew out of the ritual one.

お祝い covers everything from a wedding gift to a passing-the-exam dinner to the red envelope of money you hand someone at New Year's. お祝いを贈る (send a celebratory gift). お祝いの言葉 (words of congratulation). 合格のお祝いに食事に行こう (let's go eat to celebrate passing). It's both the act and the gift — context sorts out which.

Written in kanji as 櫛 — one of those characters so complicated that almost nobody writes it. The kanji packs 木 (wood), 節-related elements (joints/teeth), and strokes suggesting something with many parallel tines. A perfect picture of what it is: a wooden object with teeth. But in practice, you'll see this word in hiragana.

There's a superstition around くし as a gift: don't give one. The sounds く and し can be heard as 苦 (suffering) and 死 (death). This kind of phonetic taboo runs deep in Japanese culture — the number four (し) gets avoided in hospitals for the same reason. A comb is a perfectly normal object to own, but handing one to someone as a present is like giving a clock in Chinese culture: the sound curses the gesture.

Things spill themselves in Japanese. こぼれる is intransitive — the water spills, the rice falls off the spoon, the tears overflow. Nobody is doing it. It just happens. コーヒーがこぼれた (the coffee spilled). If you need to blame someone, reach for the transitive partner こぼす: コーヒーをこぼした (I spilled the coffee).

The word has a surprisingly poetic second life. 涙がこぼれる — tears spill, tears overflow. Not crying on purpose, not weeping dramatically. Tears that escape on their own, that you couldn't hold back. 笑みがこぼれる — a smile spills out, breaks through despite yourself. In both cases, the intransitivity is the point: these aren't things you did. They happened to you. The emotion was too full and it overflowed.

Particles: こぼれる takes が — the spilling thing is the subject, not an object. 水がこぼれた (the water spilled). Use から for what it spills from: コップから水がこぼれた (water spilled from the glass).

Passive: Rare — since the verb is already intransitive and describes something happening on its own, passivizing it is unusual. You'd more likely use こぼす (transitive) in passive: コーヒーをこぼされた (someone spilled coffee on me).

Causative を／にこぼれさせる: making something spill. を for coercive — but in practice you'd almost always just use こぼす instead.

一段 Ichidan自動詞 — pair: こぼす
Dictionaryこぼれるが
Negativeこぼれないが
Politeこぼれますが
Polite negこぼれませんが
Pastこぼれたが
Polite pastこぼれましたが
て-formこぼれてが
Volitional
Polite vol
Passive
Causativeこぼれさせるを／に
Caus-passこぼれさせられるに
Potential
Imperative
Imp なさい
Cond ～ばこぼれればが
Cond ～たらこぼれたらが

伝える is what you do when something needs to get from one person to another — a message, a feeling, a tradition. Not "say" (that's 言う) and not "teach" (that's 教える). 伝える is about the transmission — making sure something crosses the gap between people. 気持ちを伝える (convey your feelings). 彼にメッセージを伝えてください (please pass the message to him).

The kanji 伝 is 亻 (person) plus 云, which originally meant clouds drifting. A person passing something along like clouds drifting across the sky — things that move from here to there. The same kanji gives you 伝統 (でんとう, tradition — things passed down), 伝説 (でんせつ, legend — a story passed along), and 伝染 (でんせん, contagion — disease passing between people). The thread is always the same: something moves from source to receiver.

Its intransitive partner 伝わる (つたわる) flips the perspective: the message arrives rather than being sent. 気持ちが伝わった (the feelings got through). You tried to 伝える; did it 伝わる?

Particles: 伝える takes を for the content being conveyed and に for the recipient. 彼にニュースを伝える (convey the news to him).

Potential が伝えられる: が marks what can be conveyed. 言葉で伝えられないこともある (some things can't be conveyed with words).

Passive に伝えられる: に marks who does the conveying. 先輩に伝えられた話 (a story told to me by my senior). Also commonly used for traditions: 代々伝えられてきた (passed down through generations).

Causative に…を伝えさせる: に marks who you have convey the message, を marks the content. 部下にニュースを伝えさせた (had my subordinate deliver the news).

一段 Ichidan他動詞 — pair: 伝わる (つたわる)
Dictionaryつたえるを
Negativeつたえないを
Politeつたえますを
Polite negつたえませんを
Pastつたえたを
Polite pastつたえましたを
て-formつたえてを
Volitionalつたえようを
Polite volつたえましょうを
Passiveつたえられるに
Causativeつたえさせるに／を
Caus-passつたえさせられるに
Potentialつたえられるが
Imperativeつたえろを
Imp なさいつたえなさいを
Cond ～ばつたえればを
Cond ～たらつたえたらを

膝 is one of those kanji that looks impossibly complicated until you break it apart: 月 (flesh/body radical) on the left tells you it's a body part, and the right side is phonetic. But forget the kanji — nobody writes it by hand. Focus on the sound: ひざ. Two syllables for one of the most culturally loaded joints in the Japanese body.

In traditional Japanese life, the knee is where you live. Sitting in 正座 (せいざ, formal kneeling) means your knees bear your full weight for the entire visit, tea ceremony, or meal. 膝が痛い (my knees hurt) is something you'll hear from anyone who's spent an hour on a tatami floor. The phrase 膝を付く (to kneel, literally "attach one's knees") carries weight — it implies submission, formality, or real physical effort.

There's also 膝の上 — on one's lap. A child sitting on your lap is on your 膝. The knee and the lap are the same word because, in a seated position, your knees are your lap.

肘 — flesh radical (月) on the left, 九 (nine) on the right. Why nine? The elbow bends at about a 90-degree angle — 九十度 (90 degrees) starts with nine. That's probably folk etymology rather than historical fact, but it's a useful mnemonic: nine makes an angle, elbows make angles.

肘をつく (prop your elbow on the table) is considered bad manners in Japan, just like in many cultures. 肘掛け椅子 (ひじかけいす, armchair — literally "elbow-resting chair") gives you the word's most common compound.

Notice how ひざ (knee) and ひじ (elbow) sound almost identical — hi-za vs hi-ji. Both start with ひ, both are two-syllable body-part words. Mix-ups are common among learners. The trick: ざ has a harder buzz (think "za" = ground, knees touch the ground), じ is softer ("ji" = above, elbows stay up).

The kanji 枕 is 木 (wood) plus 冘. Traditional Japanese pillows weren't the soft poufs Westerners imagine — they were hard wooden or ceramic blocks, sometimes with a small cushion on top, designed to keep your elaborate hairstyle intact while sleeping. The wooden radical isn't metaphorical. These things were literally made of wood.

If you know any classical Japanese literature, the word 枕 might ring a bell: 枕草子 (まくらのそうし, The Pillow Book), written around the year 1000 by Sei Shonagon. It's one of the oldest works of Japanese prose — a collection of observations, lists, and complaints kept by a woman at the imperial court. The "pillow" in the title probably refers to notes kept by the bedside, jotted down before sleep.

枕が変わると眠れない (when the pillow changes, I can't sleep) — a common Japanese saying about being unable to sleep somewhere unfamiliar. The pillow stands in for the whole bed, the whole room, the whole feeling of home.

Walk through any Japanese city and you'll notice something: buildings get described before they get named. あの白い建物 (that white building). 駅の前の建物 (the building in front of the station). Japanese addresses don't always have street names the way Western ones do, so buildings become landmarks, and 建物 is the word that turns them into reference points.

The compound is transparent: 建 (to build, from 建てる) + 物 (thing). A built thing. It's the all-purpose word — an apartment, a skyscraper, a shed, a temple. 建物 doesn't care about size or prestige. Compare ビル (from English "building"), which specifically means a multi-story commercial structure. You'd call Tokyo Tower a 建物 but never a ビル. A tiny neighborhood shrine is a 建物 too.

Two kanji that both mean "road" pressed together: 道 (みち, the path, the way) and 路 (ろ, a route or road). This kind of synonym-doubling is common in Chinese-origin compounds — the redundancy adds formality. 道路 is the official, infrastructure-grade word for roads. Governments build 道路. Traffic reports talk about 道路. Signs say 道路工事中 (road construction in progress).

Compare: 道 alone is warmer, more human-scale. A path through the woods, a street you walk down, the "way" of life. 通り (とおり) is a named street in a city. 道路 is asphalt and engineering — the physical infrastructure. 道路が混んでいる (the road is congested) sounds natural. 道が混んでいる works too but feels more casual. 高速道路 (こうそくどうろ, expressway) is one of the most common compounds.

Four corners. 四つ (four) + 角 (corner, angle). Where two roads cross, four corners appear. It's a wonderfully literal description of an intersection — you stand at one corner and see three others.

When giving directions in Japanese, 四つ角 is a key landmark word. 次の四つ角を右に曲がってください (turn right at the next intersection). Other direction-giving words: 曲がり角 (corner where the road bends), 信号 (traffic light), 橋 (bridge). Japanese directions tend to rely on landmarks rather than street names, since many streets in Japan don't have names the way Western streets do.

The 角 kanji is versatile: 角度 (かくど, angle), 三角 (さんかく, triangle), 角 (つの, horn). Corner, angle, horn — all things that stick out at a point.

曲がり (a bend, from 曲がる = to turn/bend) + 角 (corner). The corner where the road bends. Unlike 四つ角 (a four-way intersection), 曲がり角 is where the road itself changes direction — a turn, a bend, a corner you go around.

It also works figuratively: 人生の曲がり角 (a turning point in life). The metaphor is vivid — you can't see what's around the corner. Life bends, and you follow it into the unknown.

In giving directions: あの曲がり角を曲がって (turn at that corner). This creates the pleasant redundancy of using 曲がる and 曲がり角 together — bend at the bending-corner. It sounds natural in Japanese.

警 means to warn or admonish. 察 means to inspect or observe. Together: the people who warn and watch. The compound captures both halves of policing — deterrence and surveillance.

警察を呼ぶ (call the police) — the phrase you hope you'll never need but should absolutely know. 警察官 (けいさつかん) is the formal word for a police officer. In casual speech, people say お巡りさん (おまわりさん) — a friendly, almost affectionate term — or just 警察 to refer to the institution and its people interchangeably.

Japanese police have a distinctive institution: 交番 (こうばん), the neighborhood police box. These small stations dot every few blocks in urban Japan, staffed by officers who handle everything from lost umbrellas to directions. The word 交番 was even borrowed into English by international policing organizations as a model for community-based law enforcement.

空 (empty, sky) + 気 (spirit, energy, air). Empty energy — the invisible stuff that fills the space around you. The literal meaning is straightforward: the air we breathe. 空気がきれい (the air is clean). 空気を入れる (let air in / air out a room).

But the word you really need to know is the figurative one: 空気を読む (to read the air). This is one of the most important social skills in Japanese culture. It means sensing the unspoken mood of a room — knowing what people are thinking without them saying it, adjusting your behavior accordingly. Someone who can't 空気を読む is called KY (from 空気読めない, can't read the air) — one of the more damning social labels in Japan.

空気 in this figurative sense is what English might call "the vibe" or "the room" — but in Japanese it's more codified, more expected. Not reading the air isn't just a social misstep; it's a failure of empathy. The word carries the full weight of Japan's indirect communication culture.

From English "sports," borrowed with the plural-ish form intact but used as a singular uncountable concept. スポーツをする (to do sports / play sports) — note: する, not やる, is the standard pairing here, though やる works too in casual speech.

Japanese uses スポーツ where English might say "athletics" or "exercise" more broadly. スポーツの秋 (autumn of sports) is a set phrase — Japan's fall season is associated with outdoor athletic activities, partly because Sports Day (スポーツの日, a national holiday) falls in October. The word has a healthy, wholesome connotation that the English "sports" doesn't always carry.

One common compound: スポーツ選手 (athlete, literally "sports player"). And スポーツカー (sports car) — loanwords cheerfully compound with other loanwords in katakana.

Borrowed from English "chance" but the meaning narrowed on arrival. In English, "chance" can mean probability, luck, risk, or opportunity. In Japanese, チャンス almost always means opportunity — a favorable opening you should seize. チャンスを逃す (miss a chance). 今がチャンスだ (now's the opportunity).

For "chance" in the sense of probability or randomness, Japanese uses 可能性 (かのうせい) or 確率 (かくりつ). For luck, 運 (うん). チャンス is always positive — it's the window that opens, the moment to act. You wouldn't say チャンスがある to mean "there's a risk." It's pure upside.

ピンチはチャンス — a crisis is an opportunity. It's practically a slogan in Japanese motivational culture, and you'll hear it in sports commentary, business meetings, and pep talks.

From English "cleaning," but it doesn't mean general cleaning in Japanese. クリーニング specifically means dry cleaning — the professional service. クリーニングに出す (send to the dry cleaner's) is the standard phrase. The verb is 出す (to put out, to send) because you're handing your clothes over to someone else.

For regular cleaning — mopping the floor, tidying the kitchen — Japanese uses 掃除 (そうじ) or 片付ける (かたづける). For laundry you do yourself, 洗濯 (せんたく). クリーニング occupies a very specific slot: professional garment care.

This loanword took a detour on its way into Japanese. Beyond the English meaning of "service" (customer service, a service you provide), サービス developed an extra meaning: something given for free, a freebie, an extra thrown in as a bonus. これはサービスです at a restaurant means "this is on the house." サービスで付けておきます (I'll throw it in for free).

The logic of the drift makes sense: good service means giving the customer something extra. Over time, the "extra" part became the whole meaning. In a shop, サービス品 means a discounted or bonus item. サービスする (as a する-verb) can mean to give a discount or add something gratis.

The original "service" meaning exists too — サービスが良い (the service is good) works exactly like English. You just have to be aware that the same word might mean "freebie" in a different context.

Straight from English "group" with no significant meaning shift. グループに分かれる (split into groups). グループ活動 (group activity). Clean and predictable.

The interesting thing about グループ is what it replaces — or doesn't replace. Japanese has native words for collections of people: 仲間 (なかま, companions/crew) implies emotional closeness. 集団 (しゅうだん) is a formal "group" with connotations of organized mass behavior. 団体 (だんたい) is an organization or association. グループ is neutral — no warmth, no formality, no organizational implication. Just a set of people or things lumped together. That neutrality is why it's used so broadly: school groups, work groups, music groups, discussion groups.

自 (self) + 宅 (residence) = one's own home. This is the word news anchors use: 容疑者は自宅で逮捕された (the suspect was arrested at his home). It's formal, objective, and neutral — it refers to someone's residence without the warmth of うち or the physical structure connotation of 家 (いえ).

自宅で仕事をする (work from home) became ubiquitous vocabulary in recent years. The compound 在宅 (ざいたく, being at home) uses the same 宅 and shows up in 在宅勤務 (ざいたくきんむ, remote work).

The kanji 宅 is the roof radical 宀 over 乇. It's the formal, somewhat bureaucratic word for a dwelling — used in addresses (お宅, your home — polite), delivery services, and official documents. 自宅 is what you write on forms. うち is what you say to friends.

家 (house) + 庭 (garden) = house and garden, the complete domestic picture. But 家庭 doesn't mean a building with a yard. It means the family unit and the domestic sphere they inhabit — closer to "household" or "home life" than to "house."

家庭の事情 (family circumstances) is the polite, vague way Japanese explains personal problems — financial trouble, divorce, illness — without specifying what's wrong. It's a socially accepted black box. 家庭科 (かていか, home economics) is the school subject where students learn cooking and sewing. 家庭教師 (かていきょうし, private tutor — literally "home teacher").

Compared to 家族 (かぞく), which means the people in the family, 家庭 includes the people and the domestic environment. A dysfunctional 家庭 doesn't just mean bad relatives — it means the whole home situation is troubled.

期 (a set time, deadline) + 間 (interval, space between) = a defined stretch of time with a beginning and end. Not vague "time" — 期間 always implies boundaries. A contract has a 期間. A sale runs for a 期間. A visa has a 期間.

期間限定 (きかんげんてい, limited-time) — you'll see this everywhere in Japan. Limited-time flavors at convenience stores, seasonal menus at restaurants, special edition products. The phrase creates urgency: this exists for a 期間, then it's gone. Japanese consumer culture runs on this concept.

試用期間 (しようきかん, trial period). 保証期間 (ほしょうきかん, warranty period). 長期間 (ちょうきかん, long period). Every 期間 compound reinforces the same idea: time with walls around it.

Japan's year doesn't start when you think it does. The 年度 — the official year for business, government, and school — runs from April 1 to March 31. Cherry blossoms mark the beginning, not the end. New employees start in April. School starts in April. Government budgets reset in April. If someone says 今年度 (こんねんど, this fiscal year) in February, they mean the year that started last April.

年 (year) + 度 (degree, time, measure) = a measured year, a year defined by institutional purpose rather than the calendar. 2024年度 means the fiscal/academic year starting April 2024. 年度末 (ねんどまつ, end of the fiscal year — March) is crunch time in offices across Japan: budgets get spent, reports get filed, people scramble.

This word matters because so much of Japanese scheduling revolves around 年度 rather than the calendar year. Knowing that April, not January, is the real starting line explains a lot about how Japan organizes itself.

経 means to pass through. 験 means to test or verify. Together: what you gain from passing through something — experience. Not abstract knowledge but the kind of understanding that only comes from having been there and done it.

経験する is the する-verb form: 海外生活を経験する (experience living abroad). But 経験 as a noun is even more common: 経験がある (have experience), いい経験になった (it was a good experience). That second phrase is worth memorizing — Japanese speakers reach for it constantly, especially when something was difficult but valuable.

経験 shows up on every Japanese resume. 経験者優遇 (けいけんしゃゆうぐう, experienced candidates preferred) is standard in job postings. 未経験 (みけいけん, no experience) marks the other side. In the Japanese workplace, what you've done matters at least as much as what you've studied.

安 (peaceful, cheap, ease) + 全 (complete, whole) = completely at peace. No danger, no risk, everything intact. The word covers both the state (safety) and the quality (safe): 安全な場所 (a safe place), 安全を守る (protect safety).

Japan is famously obsessed with 安全. Construction sites have 安全第一 (あんぜんだいいち, safety first) banners everywhere. Train announcements repeat safety reminders. Food packaging lists allergens in exhaustive detail. The culture treats safety not as a baseline but as an active, ongoing project.

Pair it with its opposite: 危険 (きけん, danger) — the next word in this list. 安全 and 危険 are two sides of the same coin and frequently appear together: 安全か危険か (safe or dangerous?).

Dictionaryあんぜん
Negativeあんぜんじゃない
Pastあんぜんだった
Past negあんぜんじゃなかった
Adverbあんぜんに
て-formあんぜんで

危 is a person crouching on a cliff edge — the kanji itself looks precarious. 険 means steep or perilous, with the hill radical 阝 on the left. Together: clinging to a precipice. The visual is baked into the characters.

危険 works as both a noun and a な-adjective. 危険な目に遭う (encounter a dangerous situation). 危険を冒す (take a risk). 命に危険がある (there is danger to life). Signs screaming 危険！ appear near construction sites, electrical equipment, cliffs — anywhere injury is possible.

There's a useful shortened form: 危ない (あぶない), the い-adjective version of "dangerous." 危険 is the formal, official word — the one on warning labels. 危ない is what you shout when someone is about to step into traffic. Same concept, different registers.

Dictionaryきけん
Negativeきけんじゃない
Pastきけんだった
Past negきけんじゃなかった
Adverbきけんに
て-formきけんで

注 (to pour) + 意 (mind, intention) = to pour your mind into something. That's attention at its most visceral — not passive awareness but actively directing your mental energy somewhere.

The word does quadruple duty. As a warning: 注意！足元に気をつけて (caution! watch your step). As attention: 注意を払う (pay attention). As a scolding: 先生に注意された (the teacher warned/scolded me). As care: 注意深い (careful, cautious).

That third use — being scolded — catches learners off guard. 注意する when directed at a person doesn't mean "to pay attention to them." It means "to caution them, to call them out, to give them a talking-to." 子供を注意する = scold the child. 子供に注意する = pay attention to the child. The particle makes the difference: を makes them the target of correction, に makes them the object of your focus.

In Japanese, you don't just "achieve" success — you harvest it. 成功を収める (achieve success) uses the verb 収める, which literally means to reap or gather in. Success is a crop. You plant effort, you tend it, you wait, and eventually you harvest 成功. The agricultural metaphor is telling: this culture doesn't believe in overnight success.

成功 pairs naturally with 努力 (effort) — in Japanese thinking, the two are practically inseparable. 努力なしに成功はない (there is no success without effort) isn't just a proverb; it's a cultural assumption. 実験が成功した (the experiment succeeded). 大成功 (たいせいこう, great success).

Its opposite is 失敗 (しっぱい, failure), and the two constantly orbit each other: 成功も失敗も経験だ (both success and failure are experience). The kanji themselves tell the story: 成 (to accomplish) + 功 (merit) — achievement through earned merit, not luck.

If there's one word that captures the Japanese work ethic in two syllables, it might be 努力. 努 means to exert oneself (it contains 力, power, at the bottom — power being squeezed out). 力 is force. Together: the application of sustained force toward a goal.

Japanese culture values 努力 as a near-universal explanation for outcomes. Talent matters, but effort is what gets respect. 努力すれば必ずできる (if you put in the effort, you can definitely do it) is the kind of thing teachers, parents, and coaches say constantly. Whether or not it's always true, the belief in 努力 as the path to success is foundational.

努力家 (どりょくか, a hard worker — a person defined by their effort) is always a compliment. もっと努力しなさい (try harder) is what you hear when you're falling short. The word carries both encouragement and pressure in equal measure.

Buy any appliance in Japan and it comes with a 説明書 (せつめいしょ, instruction manual) that could double as a novella. Pages of diagrams, warnings, edge cases. That thoroughness is 説明 in its natural habitat — the Japanese instinct to explain completely, leaving no gap for misunderstanding.

説明する is one of the most-used する-verbs in classrooms, meetings, and customer service. もう一度説明してください (please explain one more time). The kanji paint a picture of what good explanation does: 説 (to explain) + 明 (bright, clear) — words that bring light into a dark room.

Watch the grammar closely: Xについて（人）に説明する — the person you explain to takes に, the topic takes について or を. 学生にルールを説明する (explain the rules to the students). Getting these particles right is the difference between being understood and causing the very confusion you were trying to clear up.

地 (ground, earth) + 震 (to shake, tremble). The ground shakes. No metaphor, no abstraction — the word is a statement of physical fact.

If you live in or visit Japan, this is survival vocabulary. Japan experiences thousands of earthquakes per year, most too small to notice, some devastating. 地震が来た (an earthquake came) — note the verb: earthquakes come in Japanese, like a visitor arriving. 大地震 (だいじしん or おおじしん, a big earthquake). 震度 (しんど, seismic intensity) is the Japanese scale you'll hear on every earthquake broadcast — it measures how much the ground shakes at a specific location, from 1 (barely felt) to 7 (catastrophic).

The kanji 震 is 雨 (rain) over 辰 (dragon). In ancient Chinese mythology, dragons caused storms and earthquakes — heavenly beasts whose movements made the earth tremble. The character preserves that old belief: rain and dragons, the powers that shake the world.

手 (hand) + 術 (technique, art). Surgery as a hand-art — the technique of the hands. 術 is the same kanji in 技術 (ぎじゅつ, technology/skill) and 芸術 (げいじゅつ, art). It always implies mastery of a craft.

手術を受ける (to undergo surgery — literally "receive" it) is the standard patient-side phrase. 手術する is what the doctor does. 手術室 (しゅじゅつしつ, operating room). 手術が成功した (the surgery was successful).

Pronunciation note: しゅじゅつ is a tongue-twister for many learners. The じゅ in the middle gets swallowed easily. Practice it slowly: shu-ju-tsu. In fast speech, native speakers sometimes blur it to something like しじつ, but you should aim for the full form.

The kanji tell you everything: 火 (fire) + 傷 (wound, injury). A fire-wound. A burn. But the reading やけど comes from the native Japanese verb 焼ける (やける, to burn/be burned) — やけど is the noun form of what happens when you get burned.

火傷をする (to get burned) — note that you "do" a burn to yourself, using する. 熱いお茶で火傷した (I burned myself with hot tea). It covers everything from a minor kitchen scalding to a serious injury.

There's an idiom worth knowing: 大火傷をする in a figurative sense means to suffer a major setback — to "get burned" in business, a relationship, or a risky venture. Same metaphor as English, same visceral punch.

課 (lesson, section, to assign) + 題 (topic, title, problem). An assigned topic — something handed to you that needs solving or completing. In school: homework, a class project, a research assignment. In the workplace: a pending task, an unsolved problem, an area for improvement.

The workplace sense is the one that gives 課題 its real weight. 今後の課題 (challenges going forward) is a phrase you'll hear at the end of every Japanese business presentation. It means "things we still need to figure out" — a formal way of acknowledging that the work isn't done. 課題を解決する (solve a problem/task). 大きな課題が残っている (a major challenge remains).

Compare with 宿題 (しゅくだい, homework), which is specifically schoolwork assigned to take home. 課題 is broader — any assigned challenge, in any context.

You already know 子供 (こども, child). 子 by itself is shorter, warmer, and more versatile. いい子 (a good kid). あの子 (that kid/that girl). 男の子 / 女の子 (boy/girl). It's affectionate — calling someone 子 implies smallness, youth, or endearment.

But 子's real power is as a suffix. It attaches to words to create names for living things, concepts, or even people. 子猫 (こねこ, kitten). 子犬 (こいぬ, puppy). The 子 means "young one." In personal names, it's the most common ending for women's names — 花子, 洋子, 京子 — though this trend peaked mid-century and is now less dominant among younger generations.

The kanji itself is one of the simplest: a picture of a baby with outstretched arms. The horizontal stroke is the arms, the vertical stroke is the body, the bottom hook is the swaddling cloth. It's been a baby for 3,000 years.

確 (certain, sure) + 認 (to recognize, acknowledge). To make something certain by recognizing it — verification. This is the word of Japanese conscientiousness.

You will hear 確認してください (please confirm/verify) in every professional setting: offices, airports, hospitals, restaurants. Japanese work culture runs on double-checking. Before the train departs, the conductor does 指差し確認 (しさしかくにん, point-and-confirm) — physically pointing at each signal and gauge while calling out its status. This system, invented by Japanese railways, has been adopted worldwide because it dramatically reduces human error.

予約の確認 (reservation confirmation). もう一度確認する (check one more time). 確認が取れた (confirmation has been obtained). The word is everywhere because the habit of verifying is everywhere.

実 (fruit, truth, reality) + 際 (occasion, boundary, actual). Reality at the boundary — what you find when you get close enough to see what's really there, past the assumptions.

実際 is the pivot word that flips expectations. Whatever came before it was theory, rumor, or assumption. Whatever comes after is what's actually true. 簡単そうだけど、実際は難しい (it looks easy, but in reality it's hard). 実際に会ってみたら、いい人だった (when I actually met them, they were nice).

It works both as a noun (実際のところ, in actuality) and as an adverb (実際に, actually, in practice). The に form is more common in conversation. You'll reach for this word whenever you need to contrast what people think with what's real — which, in any language, is surprisingly often.

In the 1980s, Japan was gripped by a buzzword: 国際化 (こくさいか, internationalization). The country was becoming an economic superpower and needed to engage with the world. Politicians said it. Businesspeople said it. Schools launched English programs in its name. Whether the project succeeded is still debated, but the word 国際 embedded itself permanently in the vocabulary.

国際 works like a prefix — it doesn't stand alone the way English "international" does. It bolts onto nouns: 国際空港 (international airport), 国際電話 (international phone call), 国際関係 (international relations). To make it a full adjective, add 的: 国際的な問題 (an international issue).

The kanji: 国 (country) + 際 (boundary, between). The same 際 from 実際 — there it means "at the edge of reality," here it means the space where countries meet. Borders are where the interesting things happen.

会 (to meet) + 議 (to discuss, deliberate). People meeting to discuss things. That's a meeting. The compound is formal and workplace-specific — you wouldn't use 会議 for getting together with friends.

会議中 (かいぎちゅう, in a meeting) is the Japanese equivalent of a closed door. It means "do not disturb." 会議に出る (attend a meeting). 会議を開く (hold a meeting — literally "open" one). 会議室 (かいぎしつ, meeting room/conference room).

Japanese meetings have a reputation for being long and consensus-driven. The concept of 根回し (ねまわし, behind-the-scenes groundwork) means that by the time the formal 会議 happens, the real decisions have often already been made through informal conversations. The meeting itself is where the agreement gets formalized and everyone nods together. Understanding this helps you understand why 会議 in Japan can feel ritualistic rather than deliberative.

After all the 根回し (behind-the-scenes groundwork) is done, someone still has to formally put the idea on the table. That's 提案. 会議で新しいプランを提案した (I proposed a new plan at the meeting). いい提案がある (I have a good suggestion).

It sits in a middle register: more formal than アイデア (a casual idea you toss out) but less weighty than 提言 (ていげん, a formal recommendation from an expert body). 提案 is what you do in a meeting — you present a worked-out suggestion for others to consider.

The kanji 案 has a lovely image buried in it: 木 (tree) over 安 (peace). Sitting peacefully under a tree — the place where ideas come to you. Newton had his apple tree; Japanese has it baked into the character for "plan." The same 案 appears in 案内 (あんない, guidance) and 答案 (とうあん, answer sheet).

事務 (business affairs, clerical work) + 所 (place). The place where business gets done. Not a corner office in a skyscraper — 事務所 is more modest than that. A law office. A talent agency. A local government office. The word implies a working space where paperwork and administrative tasks happen.

事務所に戻る (return to the office). 弁護士事務所 (べんごしじむしょ, law office). 芸能事務所 (げいのうじむしょ, talent agency) — in the Japanese entertainment industry, 事務所 is the standard word for the agency that manages celebrities.

Compare: オフィス (from English "office") tends to refer to modern corporate workspaces, especially in tech and international business. 事務所 is broader and more traditional — the default Japanese word for any working office.

教 (to teach) + 授 (to give, bestow, confer). Teaching as a bestowal — not just passing along information but conferring knowledge with authority. The word carries a weight that the casual English "professor" sometimes doesn't.

In Japanese academia, 教授 is a specific rank — the highest regular faculty position, above 准教授 (じゅんきょうじゅ, associate professor) and 助教 (じょきょう, assistant professor). You don't call every university teacher 教授 — that would be like calling every doctor a surgeon. When addressing a professor, the title comes after the name: 田中教授. Or simply 教授 by itself, the way English speakers might say "Professor."

As a する-verb, 教授する means "to teach" in a formal, somewhat archaic sense — to instruct with expertise. You're more likely to see this in writing than to hear it in conversation.

世 (generation, world, era) + 紀 (chronicle, era, record). A recorded era — a century. 21世紀 (にじゅういっせいき, the 21st century). Simple, clean, works the same way as English.

But 世紀 has a dramatic second life in the phrase 世紀の — "of the century," meaning once-in-a-lifetime, era-defining. 世紀の大発見 (discovery of the century). 世紀の大事件 (incident of the century). Japanese media loves this construction. Anything sufficiently dramatic gets the 世紀の stamp.

The kanji 紀 contains the thread radical 糸 — the idea of spinning out a thread of time, weaving the chronicle. 世 originally depicted three sets of ten (三十), meaning a generation — roughly thirty years. Three characters that tell the story of time passing and being recorded.
